title: Equivalent Expressions
why: Two expressions are equivalent when they always give the same value. To test that, you substitute a number into each and check they match. So this lesson runs on plugging a number into a box and on knowing order doesn't change a sum or product. Warm those up first.
skills: Substituting a number for a box

diagnostic.q: Do 2 + 3 and 3 + 2 give the same value?

learn.heading: Do they always match?
learn.intro: Two expressions are equivalent if they give the same value for every number you try. A quick check: pick a number, put it in both, and see if the results are equal.
examples: x + 2 and 2 + x: try x = 4 → 4 + 2 = 6 and 2 + 4 = 6. They match — equivalent.
x + x and 2 × x: try x = 3 → 3 + 3 = 6 and 2 × 3 = 6. They match — equivalent.

tiers.intro: Put the number in the box, one step at a time.
Plug in and compare both sides.

items.hint: 2 + 5 = 7 — matches part A, so x+2 and 2+x are equivalent.

items.q: C. Since both gave 7, are x + 2 and 2 + x equivalent?

tiers.intro: Warm up straight into testing equivalence.

items.q: A. x = 4. What is x + x?
B. x = 4 gives x + x = 8 and 2 × x = 8. Are x + x and 2x equivalent?

exit.q: 2. Do 3 + x and x + 3 give the same value?

opts.t: Yes — order doesn't change a sum

exit.hint: Adding in any order gives the same total, so yes.
